channel.title: Amber Badura's sports Site
channel.description: A website about sports and data

item.title: Are the Top Teams in the NHL the Most Consistent?
item.dc:creator: Amber Badura
item.description:
Every Year in the NHL, there are a handful of teams that stand out throughout the season. But as the season goes on, it raises questions of whether those teams are truly the most consistent that they can translate going into the Stanley Cup. 

The 2024-25 season shows that even the league’s top teams aren’t always as steady and consistent throughout the season, often fluctuating. With teams like the Florida Panthers proving that you don’t have to be the top team in the league to be dominant and win the cup. Raising the question. 

Are the top teams really that consistent? And is consistency really key to winning it all? 

NHL Stats shows us that even among the top teams in the NHL. That team’s defensive performance isn’t always perfectly consistent throughout the season. Teams like the Toronto Maple Leafs and the Florida Panthers are two teams that saw their goals against per game increase in the second half of the season compared to the first half.

Meanwhile, teams like the Winnipeg Jets who were 1st in the league and the Dallas Stars who were 5th in the league, experienced having a smaller shift in the first half and the second half. This shows that consistency isn’t always what determines a team’s success throughout the season.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggnewscale)

performance_1 <- read_csv ("first half - Sheet1.csv") |> mutate(TimePeriod = "First Half")
performance_2 <- read_csv("second half - Sheet1.csv") |> mutate(TimePeriod = "Second Half")
performance <- bind_rows(performance_1, performance_2)

performance$TimePeriod <- factor(performance$TimePeriod, levels=c("First Half", "Second Half"))

fp <- performance |> filter(Team == "Florida Panthers")
wj <- performance |> filter(Team == "Winnipeg Jets") 
wc <- performance |> filter(Team == "Washington Capitals")
vk <- performance |> filter(Team == "Vegas Golden Knights") 
tm <- performance |> filter(Team == "Toronto Maple Leafs")  
ds <- performance |> filter(Team == "Dallas Stars")   

teams <- bind_rows(fp, wj, wc, vk, tm, ds)
ggplot() + 
  geom_line(data=performance, aes(x=TimePeriod, y=`GA/GP`, group = Team), color="lightgrey", alpha= .4) +
  geom_point(data=performance, aes(x=TimePeriod, y=`GA/GP`), color="lightgrey", alpha= .4) +
  geom_line(data=fp, aes(x=TimePeriod, y=`GA/GP`, group = Team), color="#B9975B") +
  geom_point(data=fp, aes(x=TimePeriod, y=`GA/GP`), color="#B9975B") +
  geom_line(data=wj, aes(x=TimePeriod, y=`GA/GP`, group = Team), color="navy") +
  geom_point(data=wj, aes(x=TimePeriod, y=`GA/GP`), color="navy") +
  geom_line(data=wc, aes(x=TimePeriod, y=`GA/GP`, group = Team), color="red") +
  geom_point(data=wc, aes(x=TimePeriod, y=`GA/GP`), color="red") +
  geom_line(data=vk, aes(x=TimePeriod, y=`GA/GP`, group = Team), color="gold") +
  geom_point(data=vk, aes(x=TimePeriod, y=`GA/GP`), color="gold") +
  geom_line(data=tm, aes(x=TimePeriod, y=`GA/GP`, group = Team), color="blue") +
  geom_point(data=tm, aes(x=TimePeriod, y=`GA/GP`), color="blue") +
  geom_line(data=ds, aes(x=TimePeriod, y=`GA/GP`, group = Team), color="darkgreen") +
  geom_point(data=ds, aes(x=TimePeriod, y=`GA/GP`), color="darkgreen") +
  geom_text_repel(data=teams |> filter(TimePeriod == "First Half"), aes(x= TimePeriod, y=`GA/GP`, group=Team, label=Team), direction = "y", nudge_x = -0.1, hjust = 1, segment.color = NA) + 
  geom_text_repel(data=teams |> filter(TimePeriod == "Second Half"), aes(x= TimePeriod, y=`GA/GP`, group=Team, label=Team), direction = "y", nudge_x = 0.1, hjust = 0, segment.color = NA) +  
  labs(
    x="", 
    y="Goals Against per Game", 
    title="Do Top NHL Teams Have the Most Consistent Defense?", 
    subtitle="In the 2024-25 season, the top teams defense perform better in the second half of the season based on GA/GP. ", 
    caption="NHL.com/stats | Amber Badura"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 19, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

In the 2024-25 season we are able to see that there really isn’t just one stat that will define a team’s success throughout the season. Defensive performance and shot control can shift quite a bit throughout the season. Showing that a team’s success can’t always be based on consistent results. 

Showing that teams that don’t dominate in these areas can find ways to win and be better than the teams that are above them. Such as the Florida Panthers who were 11th in the league. So, how often are the top teams the ones that are outshooting their opponents?

Code 
shots <- read_csv("outshootbyoutshot2 - Sheet1.csv")

shotslong <- shots |> 
select(Team, `Wins Outshoot Opp`, `Wins Outshot by Opp`) |> 
  pivot_longer(
    cols=-Team, 
    names_to="type", 
    values_to="wins")

fplong <- shotslong |> filter(Team == "Florida Panthers")
wjlong <- shotslong |> filter(Team == "Winnipeg Jets") 
wclong <- shotslong |> filter(Team == "Washington Capitals")
vklong <- shotslong |> filter(Team == "Vegas Golden Knights") 
tmlong <- shotslong |> filter(Team == "Toronto Maple Leafs")  
dslong <- shotslong |> filter(Team == "Dallas Stars")   

ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data= shotslong, aes(x=reorder(Team, wins), weight= wins , fill= type)) + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("lightgrey", "darkgrey")) +
  new_scale_fill()+
  geom_bar(data= fplong, aes(x=reorder(Team, wins), weight= wins , fill= type)) + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#B9975B", "#C8102E")) +
  new_scale_fill()+ 
  geom_bar(data= wclong, aes(x=reorder(Team, wins), weight= wins , fill= type)) + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#004C97", "#AC162C")) +
  new_scale_fill()+
  geom_bar(data= wjlong, aes(x=reorder(Team, wins), weight= wins , fill= type)) + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#C8102E", "#041E42")) +
  new_scale_fill()+ 
  geom_bar(data= vklong, aes(x=reorder(Team, wins), weight= wins , fill= type)) + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#333F42", "#B4975A")) +
  new_scale_fill()+ 
  geom_bar(data= tmlong, aes(x=reorder(Team, wins), weight= wins , fill= type)) + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("darkgrey", "#00205B")) +
  new_scale_fill()+ 
   geom_bar(data= dslong, aes(x=reorder(Team, wins), weight= wins , fill= type)) + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#00843D", "#000000")) +
  new_scale_fill()+ 
  labs(
    x="", 
    y="", 
    title="Do Top NHL Teams Outshoot the Competition?", 
    subtitle="Shot differential among league leaders in the 2024-25 season ", 
    caption="NHL.com/stats | Amber Badura"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 19, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) +
  coord_flip()

Outshooting a team’s opponents is often a sign of control, but this data suggests that even the best teams don’t always dominate the shot count.  Teams like the 2025 Stanley Cup champion Florida Panthers weren’t one of the top teams in the regular season, they still found ways to outshoot many of their opponents, while teams like the Toronto Maple Leafs were often outshot. 

One factor that can help explain this is PDO, which combines a team’s shooting and save percentage. Could shifts in the PDO between the first and second half of the season help explain why some teams win when they are outshot while others win when they outshoot their opponents? 

Code 
SAT <- read_csv("nhl_logs_2025.csv") 

fpSAT <- SAT |> filter(team_name == "Florida Panthers")
wjSAT <- SAT |> filter(team_name == "Winnipeg Jets") 
wcSAT <- SAT |> filter(team_name == "Washington Capitals")
vkSAT <- SAT |> filter(team_name == "Vegas Golden Knights") 
tmSAT <- SAT |> filter(team_name == "Toronto Maple Leafs")  
dsSAT <- SAT |> filter(team_name == "Dallas Stars")   
ggplot() + 
  geom_line(data= SAT, aes(x=date, y=pdo), color="grey") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 98.2523   ) +
  geom_line(data= vkSAT, aes(x= date, y= pdo), color="#B49751") + 
  geom_line(data= tmSAT, aes(x= date, y= pdo), color="#00205B") + 
  geom_line(data= dsSAT, aes(x= date, y= pdo), color="#00843D") +
  geom_line(data= fpSAT, aes(x= date, y= pdo), color="#B9975B") +
  geom_line(data= wjSAT, aes(x= date, y= pdo), color="#AC162C") +
  geom_line(data= wcSAT, aes(x= date, y= pdo), color="#041E42") +
  facet_wrap(~team_name,  ncol = 4) +
  new_scale_fill()+ 
  labs(
    x="", 
    y="PDO", 
    title="Identifying the Top Teams in the NHL Through PDO in the 2024–25 Season", 
    subtitle="PDO (shooting % + save %) is often used to identify teams driven by strong performance \n and favorable percentages", 
    caption="NHL.com/stats | Amber Badura") +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 19, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"  ) + 
  theme_minimal()

Throughout the season we see how much a team’s performance can fluctuate as their shooting and save percentages rise and fall. Hockey Reference was able to show the sharp spikes for teams like Winnipeg Jets and Washington Capitals show moments of peak efficiency before it dips below the average line towards the end of the season. 

Looking at these stats and graphs, we area able to see that even the top teams aren’t always the most consistent teams. We can see that teams like Florida who weren’t in the top 5 teams can prove that even with fluctuations in defense, shot control, and PDO. A team can still find a way to win and beat the top 5 teams in the 2024-25 Stanley cup competition.

item.title: Why is the University of Denver Men’s Ice hockey team the team to beat?
item.dc:creator: Amber Badura
item.description:
The University of Denver men’s hockey team has set the standard in NCAA college hockey. Having 11 titles, 3 of those being in the last 4 seasons. The Pioneers have consistently found ways to outperform the top competition.

In Denver’s recent run, it is found that there is something different about the organization. Instead of fluctuating like their opponents, the pioneers have been able to find a way to stay on top. Even with the two years where they fell short. Raising the question

What separates Denver from the rest of the NCAA Division 1 hockey teams?

The NCAA shows us the Pioneers program consistently has strong seasons, showing that you don’t have to be one of the top teams to be able to have these title-winning seasons. Over the past 4 seasons, Denver has won at least 29 games in the regular season losing, no more than 12 games.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)

denver <- read_csv("Denver year stats  - Sheet1-2.csv")

goalie26 <- read_csv("ncaa_mih_goalie_team_stats_2026.csv")
goalie25 <- read_csv("ncaa_mih_goalie_team_stats_2025.csv")
goalie24 <- read_csv("ncaa_mih_goalie_team_stats_2024.csv")
goalie23 <- read_csv("ncaa_mih_goalie_team_stats_2023.csv")

goalie <- bind_rows(goalie26, goalie25, goalie24, goalie23)

player26 <- read_csv("ncaa_mih_team_stats_2026.csv")
player25 <- read_csv("ncaa_mih_team_stats_2025.csv")
player24 <- read_csv("ncaa_mih_team_stats_2024.csv")
player23 <- read_csv("ncaa_mih_team_stats_2023.csv") 
player22 <- read_csv("ncaa_mih_team_stats_2022.csv") 

player <- bind_rows(player26, player25, player24, player23, player22)

scatterplayer <- player |> select(Season, Institution, Shots)
scattergoalie <- goalie |> select(Season, Institution, GA)

scatter <- scatterplayer |> inner_join(scattergoalie) 

denver |> 
  select(Year, Record, GA, 'Final Result') |> 
  arrange(desc(Year)) |> 
  gt() |> 
  cols_label(
    GA = "Goal Average"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Is Denver the team everyone is chasing?",
    subtitle = "Three titles in five years show how the Pioneers turn regular-season dominance into postseason success."
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |>
 tab_source_note (
    source_note = ("**By:** Amber Badura  |  **Source:** denverpioneers.com/sports/mens-ice-hockey/stats/")
  ) |> 
  tab_style(
     locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
     style = list(
       cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
       cell_text(weight = "bold", size=12)
     )
   ) |>
  opt_row_striping() |> 
  opt_table_lines("none") |>
 tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_fill(color = "#BA0C2F"),
      cell_text(color = "white")
      ),
  locations = cells_body(
    rows = `Final Result` == "National Title"
  ) 
)
Is Denver the team everyone is chasing?
Three titles in five years show how the Pioneers turn regular-season dominance into postseason success.
Year Record Goal Average Final Result
2026 29-11-3 3.61 National Title
2025 31-12-1 3.95 Semifinalist
2024 32-9-3 4.59 National Title
2023 30-10 3.75 Regional Semifinals
2022 31-9-1 3.61 National Title
**By:** Amber Badura | **Source:** denverpioneers.com/sports/mens-ice-hockey/stats/

Over the last four seasons, the Pioneers have averaged about 3.9 goals per game. Consistently staying near or above the 4-goal mark. In 2024, we are able to see that their offense peaked at 4.59 goals per game, showing their ability to go above and beyond.

But even in the years when they didn’t win the National Title, they still maintained a goal average above 3.6. Highlighting the team’s consistency over the last 4 seasons. This translates to their goaltending performance against some of their Frozen Four opponents.

Code 
du <- goalie |> filter(Institution == "Denver") 
wi <- goalie |> filter(Institution == "Wisconsin") 
bu <- goalie |> filter(Institution == "Boston U.") 
ms  <- goalie |> filter(Institution == "Minnesota St.") 

ggplot() + 
  geom_line(data= goalie, aes(x=Season, y=`Save pct`, group=Institution), color="grey", alpha= .2) +
  geom_line(data= du, aes(x=Season, y=`Save pct`), color="#A89968") +
  geom_line(data= wi, aes(x=Season, y=`Save pct`), color="black") +
  geom_line(data= bu, aes(x=Season, y=`Save pct`), color="#CC0000")+
   geom_line(data= ms, aes(x=Season, y=`Save pct`), color="#F7E400") +
  labs(
    x="Year", 
    y="Goalie Save %", 
    title="Most Consistent Goaltending Among \nChampionship Competitors (Excluding 2022)", 
    subtitle="Denver’s save percentage to its championship opponents in recent title games. \n(last five years, excluding 2022)", 
    caption="Source: NCAA | Amber Badura"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16.5, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

Across the last four seasons, Denver continues to hold a steady defensive standard when they are facing some of the top teams in the league in the postseason competition. The Pioneers goaltending remains relatively stable whether winning the title or not. In 2024, they had a 4.59 goal average while having their lowest save percentage in the last four seasons.

Yet they were still able to manage to come away with the national title. Showing that during that season, they might not have had the best defense. They did have a better offense that was able to help bring them to that title. Denver also showed that from 2024 to 2026, we see a great increase in their save percentage.

In their 2026 title, the Pioneers’ goalie Johnny Hicks, a third-string goalie, stepped in and finished the season with not only a national title but also an undefeated record of 16-0-1. His performance aligns with the team’s ability to stay competitive with the number of shots that the goalies take.

Code 
duscatter <- scatter |> filter(Institution == "Denver") 
wiscatter <- scatter |> filter(Institution == "Wisconsin") 
buscatter <- scatter |> filter(Institution == "Boston U.") 
msscatter  <- scatter |> filter(Institution == "Minnesota St.") 

ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=scatter, aes(x=Shots, y= GA), color="grey", alpha= .3)+
  geom_point(data= duscatter, aes(x=Shots, y= GA), color="#A89968")+
  geom_point(data= wiscatter, aes(x=Shots, y= GA), color="black") +
  geom_point(data= buscatter, aes(x=Shots, y= GA), color="#CC0000") +
  geom_point(data= msscatter, aes(x=Shots, y= GA), color="#F7E400") +
  labs(
    x="Shots ", 
    y="Goal Average", 
    title="How Does Denver Compare to Its Frozen \nFour Competitors in Goals and Shots?", 
    subtitle="Goal average and shots across the last four seasons for Denver and its \nFinal Four opponents.", 
    caption="Source: NCAA | Amber Badura"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

Throughout the last four seasons, we have been able to see that Denver stays competitive against all its Frozen Four opponents. Being able to balance offense and defense performance. Even with the shot and goal averages shifting slightly. The pioneers still remain consistent, while some of their opponents seem to be all over the place over the four seasons.

Denver over the last four seasons has dominated. Winning 3 titles in the last four seasons. Combining consistent offense, defense, and goaltending. Whether their goal average or save percentage is higher. The pioneers continue to show that they are the team to beat. Denver has the ability to outperform the top teams in the league and take home the title year after year.
